title: Mechanical conflict resolution silently dropped a code block — merged code did not parse
summary: Conflicts from two parallel branches touching the same file were resolved mechanically (regex over diff3 markers). The lazy match swallowed part of one side's handler, leaving an unclosed block; git merged 'successfully' and main stopped compiling.
mistake: Trusting any mechanical or textual conflict resolution — regex over conflict markers, 'take both sides' concatenation, or git's own clean-looking auto-merge of adjacent hunks — without a compile/parse step before committing. Textual success is not syntactic success: git only guarantees the merge had no overlapping hunks, not that the result is a program.
instead: After ANY conflict resolution (manual, mechanical, or auto), run the compiler/parser as a hard gate before commit: tsc/eslint/py_compile/cargo check — bare, not piped. For 3-way append conflicts prefer `git checkout --conflict=diff3` plus manual keep-both editing over regex surgery. In CI, treat a red build immediately after a merge train as 'inspect the merge commit first'.
